title: Git Fundamentals - Init, Add, Commit
summary: Learn the foundations of Git version control. Understand repositories, the staging area, commits, and the basic workflow. Git is the universal version control system used in every DevOps pipeline.

resources.title: Pro Git Book - Chapter 1: Getting Started
Pro Git Book - Chapter 2: Git Basics
Git & GitHub Crash Course For Beginners

questions.explanation: 'git init' creates a new Git repository in the current directory by creating a .git subdirectory that contains all the necessary metadata and object database for the repository.

options.text: Initializes a new Git repository in the current directory
Resets the repository to its initial state

questions.questionText: What is the Git staging area (index)?
questions.explanation: The staging area (also called the index) is an intermediate area where you prepare changes before committing. 'git add' moves changes to the staging area, and 'git commit' records the staged changes.

options.text: A remote backup of your code
An intermediate area where changes are prepared before committing
A branch where you test code
The same as the working directory

questions.questionText: Which command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area?
questions.explanation: 'git status' shows which files are modified, staged, or untracked. It is one of the most frequently used Git commands.

questions.questionText: What is a good practice for writing commit messages?
questions.explanation: Good commit messages use the imperative mood (like a command), are concise but descriptive, and explain the 'why' behind changes. The convention is: short summary (50 chars), blank line, then detailed body.

options.text: Write as much detail as possible in one long line
Use past tense like 'Fixed the bug'
Start with a short imperative summary, like 'Add user authentication'

tasks.title: Create Your First Git Repository
tasks.description:
1. Configure Git (if not already done):
   ```
   git config --global user.name 'Your Name'
   git config --global user.email 'your@email.com'
   ```
2. Create a new project directory: `mkdir my-devops-project && cd my-devops-project`
3. Initialize Git: `git init`
4. Check status: `git status`
5. Create a README: `echo '# My DevOps Project' > README.md`
6. Stage it: `git add README.md`
7. Check status again and note the difference
8. Commit: `git commit -m 'Initial commit: add README'`
9. View the log: `git log`
10. Add more files, make multiple commits, and observe the history with `git log --oneline`
hints: Use 'git log --oneline --graph' for a compact visual history
Check what has changed before committing with 'git diff' (unstaged) or 'git diff --staged' (staged)

tasks.title: Practice the Git Workflow
tasks.description:
1. In your project, create a file `app.sh` with a simple script
2. Stage and commit it
3. Modify the file and use `git diff` to see changes
4. Stage the changes with `git add app.sh`
5. Use `git diff --staged` to see staged changes
6. Commit with a descriptive message
7. Create a `.gitignore` file with common patterns:
   ```
   *.log
   .env
   node_modules/
   __pycache__/
   .DS_Store
   ```
8. Create a `test.log` file and verify it is ignored with `git status`
9. View the full history: `git log --oneline --all --graph`
10. Use `git show <commit-hash>` to inspect a specific commit
hints: Use 'git add -p' to interactively stage portions of a file
The .gitignore file itself should be committed to the repository
